transmission.detail: 20d net bills; heavy supply absorbs MMF cash, widens vs OIS
20d net Notes+Bonds; duration supply pressures term premium

calendar.event: Individual income tax deadline; largest single-day drain of the year

narrative.regime_text: Receipts are outpacing spending or the impulse trend is weakening. This drains reserves and tends to coincide with tighter front-end funding.

report.framework: This report applies an operational lens consistent with the accounting of the Federal Reserve and the US Treasury as described in the Daily Treasury Statement. Government spending = the Treasury instructs the Fed to credit reserve accounts (dollars created). Taxes and tariffs = the Fed debits reserve accounts (dollars destroyed). Securities issuance = the Fed debits reserve accounts and credits securities accounts (reserve drain, not borrowing). Securities redemption = the Fed debits securities accounts and credits reserve accounts (reserve add). The deficit = net dollars added to the non-government sector = the non-government sector's net savings in dollar financial assets. The TGA = a timing buffer between spending and collection, not a constraint on the government's ability to spend.
report.executive_summary: As of 2026-03-24, the fiscal-flow regime reads **Restrictive** for the non-government sector. Net fiscal transfer momentum is **decelerating**: 5d average is $5.8bn versus prior-20d average $11.2bn. MTD net transfers are $68.4bn, with YoY comparable at -18.4%. FYTD, the government has added $931.8bn in net financial assets to the non-government sector (-18.8% YoY); this is the net dollars spent that have not yet been used to pay taxes or purchase securities. The TGA closed at $876.2bn. Treasury spending has exceeded collections recently, drawing down the TGA and crediting reserves to the banking system.
report.what_changed: Over the last 5 sessions, average net fiscal transfer was $5.8bn per day, versus $11.2bn in the prior 20-session baseline. Tax drains averaged $14.6bn versus $20.2bn, while net withdrawals averaged $27.3bn against $36.0bn. The largest daily move in the 20-day window was 2026-02-27 at $87.1bn. On balance, weekly flow momentum is softening relative to the recent baseline.
scorecard_items: MTD net fiscal transfer (spending minus taxes/tariffs): $68.4bn (-18.4% YoY comparable).
FYTD net financial assets added to non-government sector: $931.8bn (-18.8% YoY comparable).
MTD government spending (reserve credits): $508.6bn vs MTD tax/tariff drains (reserve debits): $440.2bn.
MTD household transfers (dollars created â†' households): $111.3bn (-25.4% YoY).
MTD interest income to bondholders (dollars created â†' savers): $30.1bn (+24.9% YoY).
MTD tax refunds (dollars returned â†' households): $80.6bn (+5.5% YoY).
report.drivers: Relative to the comparable month last year, the shift is primarily spend-led. Government spending (reserve credits) moved by $-72.7bn while tax and tariff drains (reserve debits) moved by $-57.3bn. The FYTD operating deficit; dollars spent by the government that have not yet been used to pay taxes. is $931.8bn, versus $1,147.2bn at this point last year. This is identically the net financial assets added to the non-government sector's savings. Tariff collections are running +239.4% above prior year at $180.2bn FYTD, now representing 6.2% of total deposit drains. Tariffs are operationally identical to taxes: they destroy dollars. When the government collects a tariff, the Fed debits the importer's bank's reserve account; those dollars cease to exist in the private sector. Monthly tariff drains are approximately 0.19x the monthly SSA payment cycle. While tariffs are not yet cancelling out SSA payments in aggregate, the daily tariff collection trend is creeping higher and worth monitoring. both are direct dollar flows between the government and households, in opposite directions. Interest payments to bondholders are running at approximately $659.1bn on a trailing 12-month basis, representing 8.4% of total government spending. This is one of the largest single fiscal injection channels: when Treasury pays interest, the Fed credits reserve accounts of bondholder banks; these are new dollars entering the private sector. As the white paper describes, a positive policy rate is effectively "basic income for those who already have money." The higher the debt/GDP, the larger the fiscal impact of rate policy. This creates a paradox: what the Fed calls "tightening" simultaneously injects more dollars into the economy through the interest income channel, partially offsetting the intended demand reduction. Total FYTD government spending is -0.1% versus prior year, broadly on a similar trajectory to FY2025 despite headlines about spending cuts. The aggregate flow data does not yet show a material reduction in the rate at which the government is crediting reserve accounts.

signals.title: Refund Season Tracker
signals.text: FYTD tax refunds issued: $223.9bn (+8.4% YoY). Refunds are government spending; the Treasury instructs the Fed to credit recipient bank reserve accounts. These are new dollars entering the private sector, making refund season the largest predictable household cash injection in Q1. FYTD pace is tracking close to prior year; broadly normal rate of household dollar injection. The trailing 4-week pace is $0.1bn vs $0.1bn same window prior year (+9.3% YoY). The recent pace has turned positive year-over-year even while the FYTD cumulative remains below prior year; this is the inflection point that matters for near-term household cash flow. The cumulative hole was dug earlier in the fiscal year; the current run-rate is now injecting dollars at a faster rate than at the same point last year.

signals.title: Government Account Series Divergence
signals.text: Net GAS issuance is $310.9bn vs $-73.5bn at this point last year (+523.0%). Large shifts in GAS often indicate extraordinary measures during debt ceiling episodes, or significant trust fund rebalancing. This divergence warrants attention in the context of any upcoming debt ceiling deadlines.

signals.title: Tariff Revenue Acceleration
signals.text: Tariff/customs receipts FYTD: $180.2bn (+239.4% YoY). Tariffs now represent 6.2% of total deposit drains. Operationally, tariffs are identical to taxes: when collected, the Fed debits the importer's bank's reserve account; those dollars are destroyed. The economy needs the government to spend or lend those dollars back into existence before they can circulate again. The DTS does not distinguish policy tariff changes from routine customs flows, but the magnitude of the YoY shift is consistent with the new tariff regime taking effect, directly reducing private-sector dollar balances.

signals.title: Interest Income Channel & Rate Paradox
signals.text: Trailing 12-month interest payments to bondholders: approximately $659.1bn, representing 8.4% of all government spending. When Treasury pays coupon interest, the Fed credits reserve accounts; this is new money entering the economy, functionally identical to any other government spending. At current policy rates, this channel operates as what Warren Mosler describes as "basic income for those who already have money." The paradox: what the Fed calls "tightening" simultaneously increases this fiscal injection. The higher the debt/GDP ratio, the larger the fiscal impact of rate policy. If rates remain elevated and the debt continues to roll at higher coupons, interest expense will continue climbing toward and potentially beyond $1 trillion annually. a massive, regressive fiscal stimulus running on autopilot through the interest income channel.

report.base_case: The current restrictive regime persists, with net transfers running below prior-year comparable pace. This setup is more likely to leave growth and liquidity mildly constrained than meaningfully boosted.
report.alt_scenario: Conditions improve if withdrawals outpace receipts and 5d net-transfer averages remain above 20d averages. Confirmation would be improving MTD and FYTD YoY deltas plus repeated positive net-transfer surprises.
report.falsification: Sustained negative YoY net-transfer prints alongside elevated receipt leakage.
forward_calendar: **2026-04-15:** Individual income tax deadline; largest single-day drain of the year
report.forward_text: The April 15 individual income tax deadline is the single largest dollar-destruction event of the year. When taxpayers pay, the Fed debits their banks' reserve accounts; typically $150-300bn in a single day. These dollars cease to exist in the private sector. Front-end rates will firm into this date as the private sector scrambles to obtain the dollars needed to meet the obligation, and ease after. We are in peak refund season (January-April). Each refund payment is government spending; the Fed credits the recipient's bank's reserve account. The pace of this dollar injection relative to prior year is a leading indicator of Q1 household cash flow and consumption.

market_map.reason: Net fiscal transfers below prior year, but partially offset by the interest income channel (~$659.1bn/yr to bondholders). The fiscal drag from slower spending is real, but the rate paradox means "tightening" simultaneously injects dollars through interest.

Heavy 20d net bill issuance ($71.3bn). When Treasury issues bills, the Fed debits reserve accounts and credits securities accounts; draining reserves from the banking system. This pushes bill rates wider vs OIS as MMFs absorb supply.

Heavy 20d coupon issuance ($139.8bn). Notes and bonds drain reserves from the banking system and add duration to private portfolios. This is not the government "borrowing"; it is a reserve drain that occurs after spending has already credited reserves. But the term premium effect is real: more duration supply pushes yields higher.
Mixed fiscal signals; no strong directional USD read from flows
Mixed reserve signals; fiscal and TGA effects partially offset

cot.narrative: S&P 500 leveraged fund positioning is **neutral** (z-score: 0.3). No strong contrarian signal from equity futures positioning.

margin.contrarian: Bearish contrarian signal — speculative leverage at extremes. Historically, margin debt rolling over from these levels precedes drawdowns.
margin.narrative: FINRA margin debt as of 2026-02 is $1253.2bn (+36.5% YoY, trend: rising). This is at **extreme leverage** levels (z-score: 1.73). Bearish contrarian signal — speculative leverage at extremes. Historically, margin debt rolling over from these levels precedes drawdowns. Elevated speculative leverage into a restrictive fiscal regime. margin-funded positions are vulnerable if fiscal injection continues declining.

fred_refs.title: Federal Government Current Expenditures: Interest Payments
fred_refs.reason: Interest income channel running ~$659.1bn/yr; the largest autopilot fiscal injection to the saver class. Track proximity to $1T.

fred_refs.title: Treasury General Account Balance
fred_refs.reason: TGA at $876.2bn. spending exceeding collections (reserve add). TGA is the timing buffer between government spending and collection.

fred_refs.title: Customs Duties Revenue
fred_refs.reason: Tariffs running +239.4% YoY; these destroy private-sector dollars identically to taxes. Verify scale against quarterly NIPA data.

fred_refs.title: Bank Reserves at the Fed
fred_refs.reason: Net fiscal transfers declining + TGA rebuild = fewer dollars in reserve accounts. Check if aggregate reserves are compressing.

fred_refs.title: Federal Surplus or Deficit
fred_refs.reason: FYTD net financial assets added to non-government: $931.8bn vs $1,147.2bn prior; the deficit is identically the non-government sector's net savings in dollar financial assets.

fred_refs.title: 4-Week Treasury Bill Rate
fred_refs.reason: Heavy bill issuance ($71.3bn in 20d) draining reserves; check if front-end rates widening as banks compete for reduced reserve balances.

fred_refs.title: Federal Debt: Total Public Debt
fred_refs.reason: Total outstanding public debt = total dollars spent by government not yet used to pay taxes. Context for GAS/extraordinary measures and the interest income channel scale.

fred_refs.title: FINRA Margin Debt. Debit Balances in Margin Accounts
fred_refs.reason: FINRA margin debt at $1253.2bn (z-score: 1.73); speculative leverage is elevated vs recent history.

caveats: Seasonal tax dates, refunds, and benefit cycles create temporary volatility in daily flow reads.
Current month is partial until month end, so MTD inferences are provisional.
FYTD reads are matched by fiscal month index for apples-to-apples YoY comparisons.
Tariff/customs categories include broader customs receipts, not only policy tariff changes; but operationally they all destroy dollars identically.
Interest payments add dollars to the private sector in aggregate, but transmission to spending demand is distribution-dependent; bondholders have a higher savings propensity than transfer recipients.

The public debt is not a liability in the household sense. It is the accounting record of net dollars the government has spent that have not yet been used to pay taxes; it equals the non-government sector's net savings of dollar financial assets.
Securities issuance is a reserve drain, not borrowing. The government spends first (credits reserves), then offers securities which debit reserves and credit securities accounts at the Fed.
report.policy_monitor:
• **Treasury auction results show weak demand at longer maturities; 2-year allotted-at-high falls to 20.78% vs historical norms near 50%, signaling potential near-term reserve drain if TGA rebuild accelerates.** This week's auction cycle (5-year, 2-year, bills across 6-week to 26-week tenors) reflects a Restrictive regime environment; the notably poor bid-to-cover on intermediate paper suggests investor hesitation at current yield levels. If Treasury responds by frontloading shorter-dated issuance to maintain funding velocity without conceding on longer rates, TGA drawdown will slow or reverse, reducing reserve injection to private sector. Watch for TGA balance statement (next 2-4 weeks) to confirm whether this auction weakness translates to a structural shift in security-supply timing rather than calendar noise. **Timing: imminent (next weekly TGA report).**

• **Iran conflict and Trump administration fuel-cost mitigation (waived gasoline regulations, USPS fuel surcharge) create upside tail risk to energy-sector excise tax receipts near-term, offset by longer-horizon tariff/customs exposure if conflict escalates into supply disruption.** Current energy tax collections benefit from elevated crude prices; regulatory waiver reduces consumer-fuel inflation pressure but does not redirect tax base. If Iran conflict persists and oil price remains elevated through Q2 2026, fuel excise and transportation-related tax receipts could run 2-5% above baseline forecast through June, providing temporary Restrictive-regime headwind (receipts outpace spending). Conversely, if broader trade tensions with China (Xi meeting May 14-15 noted in headline) trigger tariff escalation, **customs receipts will spike materially in Q2 outbound reports** (goods enter ports in advance; duties collected on DTS 30-60 days later), further tightening the fiscal drain. **Timing: near-term (April-May collections cycle) for energy; structural (May-July if tariffs implemented) for customs.**

• **Elevated front-end bill yields (6-week, 13-week, 17-week all at 3.63-3.73% investment rates) and high allotted-at-high percentages (96.91% on 17-week, 58.27% on 13-week) signal sustained demand for short-duration reserve-holding instruments; this supports the Restrictive regime narrative but masks underlying TGA demand elasticity.** If Fed maintains restrictive stance and fiscal drag continues (receipts ahead of outlays), private sector will cycle out of longer paper and concentrate in bills and floating-rate notes (FRN auction also executed today at low allotted-at-high of 55.48%, indicating preference shift). This reshuffles duration risk but does not automatically reverse reserve tightness unless TGA rebuild slows. **Watch for whether next monthly refunding announcement includes smaller note/bond issuance quotas and larger bill allocations; this would confirm structural Restrictive-regime persistence.** **Timing: imminent (refunding announcement expected early April).**
